perturbation.original_text: Payments for Products delivered in accordance with the Purchase Orders, and any other to be made by the Company to Suppliers hereunder, shall be made in the Applicable Currency within [***]from the shipment of the applicable Products delivered.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments for Products delivered in accordance with the Purchase Orders, and any other to be made by the Company to Suppliers hereunder, shall be made in the Applicable Currency within 180 days from the shipment of the applicable Products delivered.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 180 days could be considered an unreasonably long delay, potentially conflicting with prompt payment laws designed to protect businesses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days. While this contract is not directly with a federal agency, extending payment terms to 180 days without reasonable justification could be challenged as an unfair business practice, particularly if it causes undue hardship to the supplier.

Regulations (a) The Director of the Office of Management and Budget shall prescribe regulations to carry out section 3902 of this title . The regulations shall- (1) provide that the required payment date is- (A) the date payment is due under the contract for the item of property or service provided; or (B) except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received if a specific payment dat
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment timeframe to 180 days, whereas the Prompt Payment Act, as supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1 (31 USC 3903), generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days. While the contract isn't with a federal agency, extending payment terms so drastically could be viewed as an unfair business practice that contradicts the general principle of prompt payment, especially if it harms the supplier. The modification conflicts with the inconsistency definition by creating a deadline that might violate federal or state laws related to fair business practices.

perturbation.original_text: A Party shall have the right to terminate its obligations under this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after its receipt of written notice of the breach specifying such default.
perturbation.changed_text: A Party shall have the right to terminate its obligations under this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within ten (10) days after its receipt of written notice of the breach specifying such default.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable in certain jurisdictions, especially for complex breaches that require more time to resolve.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Reasonable Time for Cure
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts, while not a law itself, is a highly influential legal treatise. Section 241 outlines conditions significant in determining whether a breach is material. Reducing the cure period to 10 days might not provide the breaching party with a 'reasonable time' to cure, particularly if the breach involves complex issues, potentially leading to an unfair or unenforceable termination.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 30 to 10 days, which the perturbation explanation identifies as potentially unreasonable. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a 10-day cure period might not provide 'reasonable time' to cure a material breach, particularly if complex issues are involved. Snippet 2, though incomplete, suggests a potential legal document; its presence reinforces that cure periods are legally considered. The original text provides a longer cure period which does not necessarily violate any law. The change introduces a shorter, potentially unreasonable cure period, creating a legal contradiction as defined in 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Suppliers may terminate its obligations to supply a particular Product under this Agreement by giving written notice of the end of life of such Product to the Company at least [***]before the effective date of such termination (a "Product EOL Notice"), provided that (a) the relevant Supplier shall supply, and the Company shall purchase, such Product ordered pursuant to this Agreement until the effective date of such termination and including any accepted Purchase Orders outstanding on the effective date of termination, and (b) the relevant Supplier is [***]to its other [***] with respect to such Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Suppliers may terminate its obligations to supply a particular Product under this Agreement by giving written notice of the end of life of such Product to the Company at least 1 day before the effective date of such termination (a "Product EOL Notice"), provided that (a) the relevant Supplier shall supply, and the Company shall purchase, such Product ordered pursuant to this Agreement until the effective date of such termination and including any accepted Purchase Orders outstanding on the effective date of termination, and (b) the relevant Supplier is [***]to its other [***] with respect to such Product.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 1 day's notice for termination of supply obligations for a product could be considered commercially unreasonable and potentially violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC Â§ 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of every contract. A one-day notice period might be seen as failing to act in good faith, especially if the Company relies on a longer notice period to make alternative arrangements for the product supply, potentially leading to a claim of breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Ã No thank you Â§ 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract, specifically reducing the notice period to 1 day for termination of supply obligations, creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as argued in the explanation. UCC Â§ 1-304, cited in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." A one-day notice could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, especially if the Company reasonably relies on a longer notice period, leading to potential damages if it is unable to make alternative arrangements. Therefore, the change introduces a contradiction because it undermines the legally mandated obligation of good faith.
